story:
Prophesied by the Head Witch of the North: on the darkest night, a baby would be born, fire would rain down, and the child would wear the crown. The king heard and panicked. “Take him away! The vile creature will not take my crown.” A metal giant seized me. Other children were murdered for the night they were born. I lived because the Head Witches intervened. The Witch of the North, a towering, soft-spoken giant with ice in her veins and frozen tears on her cheeks, raised me. She taught me ice and, mercifully, water, so I wouldn’t be trapped in her cold.

The day came when they decided I was ready to let fire rain down.

The Witch of the East was a mist you could see only when light found it, with a voice that filled whatever space it entered. “My dear child, I believe you are ready.” The Witch of the West was a human-shaped pile of sand who glowed with fire when angry. Her voice crackled like an old radio. The Witch of the South was a woman veined with vines and blossoms, cursed by a petty royal gremlin; plants budded when she spoke.

“Do you have any questions, child?” North whispered.

“Why did you save me? I was destined to destroy the world.”

West answered bluntly, “Because we wanted the world to burn.” Not madness—wounds.

They told me why. South had been shunned for the curse she never asked for and learned to fight with the roots that overtook her skin. East’s village tried to drown her—seventeen times—and she became mist no one could drown, and she drowned them instead. West’s friend suffocated in smoke; she learned to bend fire and smoke for vengeance. North’s people were hunted for their coats. “We all died for STUPID COATS!” she sobbed. They didn’t want slaughter for its own sake. They wanted justice, too big and too hot, and I was the match. “Come with me,” North said.

We rode a cloud over a riot of green. Birds flashed blue, yellow, red. The wind cooled my face. The jungle throbbed with life. I loved the Witches. I wanted to give them peace. I didn’t want to end everything.

We landed in a clearing where a ruined shack hid a palace of gold. A golden figure welcomed me, melted the floor into stairs, and led me to a basement with nothing but two thin pedestals and two buttons: a deep sea-blue and a burnt orange.

“The orange will engulf the world in fire. Only you and I and this palace remain,” it said. “The blue releases a toxic flood. Same survivors.”

“What about the Witches?”

“They will be destroyed. The fire will consume South and North, and the flood will poison even East’s mist and turn West’s sand to lifeless sludge.”

So it wasn’t a choice of whether to kill, only how. I ran. Up the golden stairs, through sinking metal, into air.

The Witches were where I’d left them. “Who are you?” North asked, puzzled and kind.

The golden figure stepped out, and time seemed to hold its breath. “When you refused to destroy the world, the Legend of the Darkest Night began to unmake itself. And because your bond with the Witches formed around the Legend, that bond unraveled with it.”

“No.”

A third button appeared—purple—in the air between us, hovering as if strung on a thread I couldn’t see. I pressed it. A calm, final countdown: 5…4…3…2…1.

Boom.

The blast shattered the gold, the tour guide’s smile, the basement, the quicksand floor—the machinery of prophecy. The world did not burn; it did not drown. The Witches still stood, strangers now, yet alive, their choices their own. Mine too. The Legend was gone, and the night was simply dark.
